prompt: In this course, what distinguishes a strategist from a technician in the way each works with AI?

options.label: A
options.text: A technician writes better prompts than a strategist.
options.feedback: Prompt skill is not the differentiator; both may prompt well.

options.label: B
options.text: A strategist applies judgment to the AI output before it moves forward, while a technician focuses on producing output quickly.
options.feedback: The differentiator is judgment applied to the output, not speed or prompt craft.

options.label: C
options.text: A strategist avoids AI entirely, while a technician relies on it.
options.feedback: The strategist uses AI but evaluates what it produces.

options.label: D
options.text: A strategist always works faster than a technician.
options.feedback: Speed is the technician's emphasis; the strategist's edge is judgment.

prompt: As AI takes over more of the production of work, where does durable human value increasingly concentrate?

options.label: A
options.text: In generating output as fast as possible.
options.feedback: Generation is what AI now does cheaply, so value moves away from raw production.

options.label: B
options.text: In the judgment used to evaluate, question, and stand behind the output.
options.feedback: Judgment becomes the differentiator as production is commoditized.

options.label: C
options.text: In memorizing more facts than the AI.
options.feedback: Recall is not where human advantage concentrates.

options.label: D
options.text: In always using the most advanced model available.
options.feedback: Tool access is not the source of durable human value here.

prompt: How are System One and System Two thinking best described?

options.label: A
options.text: System One is slow and deliberate; System Two is fast and automatic.
options.feedback: That reverses the two modes.

options.label: B
options.text: System One is fast and automatic; System Two is slow and effortful.
options.feedback: System One is intuitive and quick; System Two is deliberate and effortful.

options.label: C
options.text: Both are fast and automatic.
options.feedback: System Two is effortful, not automatic.

options.label: D
options.text: Both are slow and deliberate.
options.feedback: System One is fast and automatic.

prompt: Why does fluent, well-formatted AI output tend to escape careful scrutiny?

options.label: A
options.text: Because it is usually too long to read.
options.feedback: Length is not the mechanism described.

options.label: B
options.text: Because polish removes the rough cues that normally signal a draft needs checking, so it is accepted on fast, automatic thinking.
options.feedback: Without roughness cues, System One reads finished as fine.

options.label: C
options.text: Because formatting makes errors easier to spot.
options.feedback: The opposite is true; polish hides the cues that prompt scrutiny.

options.label: D
options.text: Because System Two activates automatically for clean documents.
options.feedback: System Two is effortful and is not triggered by clean formatting.

prompt: What does the course mean by the decision quality crisis?

options.label: A
options.text: AI models are becoming less accurate over time.
options.feedback: The crisis concerns review capacity, not declining model accuracy.

options.label: B
options.text: The volume of AI-assisted work has outpaced the capacity to review it, so unchecked outputs flow into decisions.
options.feedback: It is the widening gap between AI adoption and AI review.

options.label: C
options.text: Employees are making fewer decisions than before.
options.feedback: The issue is unreviewed decisions, not fewer decisions.

options.label: D
options.text: Organizations are using too few AI tools.
options.feedback: The crisis is about review discipline, not tool quantity.

prompt: Among the biases that affect AI use, which one is specific to working with automated systems?

options.label: A
options.text: Confirmation bias.
options.feedback: Confirmation bias predates AI and applies broadly.

options.label: B
options.text: Anchoring.
options.feedback: Anchoring is a general bias, not specific to automation.

options.label: C
options.text: Automation bias.
options.feedback: Automation bias is the tendency to trust automated output more than equivalent human input.

options.label: D
options.text: Availability.
options.feedback: Availability is a general bias, not unique to AI.

prompt: Accepting an AI output mainly because it matches what you already believed is an example of which bias?

options.label: A
options.text: Confirmation bias.
options.feedback: Confirmation bias is favoring information that agrees with existing beliefs.

options.label: B
options.text: Anchoring.
options.feedback: Anchoring is over-relying on the first figure or framing encountered.

options.label: C
options.text: Automation bias.
options.feedback: Automation bias is trusting the output because a machine produced it, regardless of agreement.

options.label: D
options.text: Availability.
options.feedback: Availability is over-weighting what is surfaced first or comes to mind easily.

prompt: In the Bias Stack, what is dataset bias?

options.label: A
options.text: Bias introduced by the reader's own assumptions.
options.feedback: That is cognitive bias, a different layer.

options.label: B
options.text: Bias inherited from the composition of the data the model was trained on.
options.feedback: Dataset bias comes from what the training data over-represents or omits.

options.label: C
options.text: Bias added only at the prompting stage.
options.feedback: Prompting sits in the cognitive layer, not the dataset layer.

options.label: D
options.text: Bias that disappears once sensitive words are removed.
options.feedback: Removing obvious terms does not remove dataset or model bias.

prompt: Why can model bias persist even after an obvious sensitive attribute is removed from the data?

options.label: A
options.text: Because the model memorizes the removed attribute.
options.feedback: The mechanism is proxies, not memorization of the deleted field.

options.label: B
options.text: Because modeling choices and proxy variables can reconstruct the same pattern through correlated signals.
options.feedback: Feature selection, proxies, and optimization targets reproduce the bias indirectly.

options.label: C
options.text: Because removing data always improves fairness.
options.feedback: Removing the signal does not guarantee fairness; proxies remain.

options.label: D
options.text: Because model bias only exists in image models.
options.feedback: Model bias is not limited to one type of model.

prompt: What are the three layers of the Bias Stack, from top to bottom?

options.label: A
options.text: Prompt bias, output bias, user bias.
options.feedback: These are not the named layers.

options.label: B
options.text: Cognitive bias, dataset bias, model bias.
options.feedback: The stack runs from the human in the room, to the training data, to the modeling choices.

options.label: C
options.text: Statistical bias, sampling bias, reporting bias.
options.feedback: These are not the course's three layers.

options.label: D
options.text: Input bias, processing bias, display bias.
options.feedback: These are not the named layers.

prompt: What are the five AI Output Trust Score criteria, in order?

options.label: A
options.text: Truthfulness, source traceability, relevance, reasoning quality, risk class.
options.feedback: The rubric runs from whether the output is correct to how much it matters.

options.label: B
options.text: Relevance, tone, accuracy, length, confidence.
options.feedback: These are not the criteria.

options.label: C
options.text: Source traceability, truthfulness, risk class, relevance, reasoning quality.
options.feedback: The order is wrong.

options.label: D
options.text: Truthfulness, creativity, speed, relevance, risk class.
options.feedback: Creativity and speed are not criteria.

prompt: Under the Trust Score, which result should trigger a review before the output is used?

options.label: A
options.text: Only when every criterion is red.
options.feedback: The threshold is lower than all red.

options.label: B
options.text: Any single red, or two or more ambers.
options.feedback: The routing rule is that any red or two ambers triggers review.

options.label: C
options.text: Only when the risk class is green.
options.feedback: A green risk class would not trigger review.

options.label: D
options.text: Never, as long as the output reads well.
options.feedback: Readability is not part of the routing rule.

prompt: What is the purpose of the risk class criterion in the Trust Score?

options.label: A
options.text: To measure how fluent the writing is.
options.feedback: Fluency is not what risk class measures.

options.label: B
options.text: To weight how much the consequences of an error matter, so scrutiny scales with the stakes.
options.feedback: Risk class captures how costly a mistake would be, guiding how hard to check.

options.label: C
options.text: To count how many sources the output cites.
options.feedback: That is closer to source traceability.

options.label: D
options.text: To rate the model's overall popularity.
options.feedback: Risk class has nothing to do with popularity.

prompt: In the context of AI outputs, what is a hallucination?

options.label: A
options.text: A factual error the model flags as uncertain.
options.feedback: Hallucinations are stated confidently, not flagged as uncertain.

options.label: B
options.text: Content the model states confidently that is fabricated or unsupported.
options.feedback: A hallucination is a confidently presented fabrication.

options.label: C
options.text: A deliberate lie programmed by the developer.
options.feedback: It is a property of how the model generates text, not a programmed lie.

options.label: D
options.text: A formatting glitch in the output.
options.feedback: It concerns fabricated content, not formatting.

prompt: What is the central lesson of the Mata v. Avianca case for professionals using AI?

options.label: A
options.text: AI should never be used for any professional work.
options.feedback: The lesson is about verification, not prohibition.

options.label: B
options.text: Confident, correctly formatted citations can still be fabricated, so each must be verified against its source.
options.feedback: The case shows fabricated citations look real and must be checked.

options.label: C
options.text: Courts will always detect AI use automatically.
options.feedback: Detection is not the lesson; the responsibility to verify is.

options.label: D
options.text: Formatting a citation correctly proves that it is real.
options.feedback: Format is exactly what cannot be trusted as proof.

prompt: What is a premortem?

options.label: A
options.text: A review held after a project fails in order to assign blame.
options.feedback: That is a postmortem, held after the fact.

options.label: B
options.text: An exercise in which the team assumes the plan has already failed and works backward to generate the reasons why.
options.feedback: A premortem uses prospective hindsight before the decision is locked.

options.label: C
options.text: A status meeting to celebrate early wins.
options.feedback: It is a risk-surfacing exercise, not a celebration.

options.label: D
options.text: A financial audit of past spending.
options.feedback: It concerns future failure modes, not past spending.

prompt: How does a red-team review differ from a premortem?

options.label: A
options.text: A red-team assumes failure has happened, while a premortem argues the plan should proceed.
options.feedback: That reverses the two techniques.

options.label: B
options.text: A premortem collaboratively imagines failure and works back to causes, while a red-team adversarially challenges whether the plan should proceed at all.
options.feedback: Premortem is prospective hindsight; red-team is adversarial pressure.

options.label: C
options.text: They are identical techniques with different names.
options.feedback: They are complementary but distinct.

options.label: D
options.text: A red-team can only be run after launch.
options.feedback: It challenges the plan before it is locked in.

prompt: What is a base rate, and which view does it represent?

options.label: A
options.text: The cost of running an AI model, representing the inside view.
options.feedback: A base rate is not a cost figure.

options.label: B
options.text: The rate at which similar past cases turned out a given way, representing the outside view.
options.feedback: Base rates draw on comparable past cases, which is the outside view.

options.label: C
options.text: The confidence the AI expresses, representing the model's view.
options.feedback: A stated confidence is not a base rate.

options.label: D
options.text: The number of steps in a plan, representing the project view.
options.feedback: That is unrelated to base rates.

prompt: What does it mean to be well calibrated in your judgments?

options.label: A
options.text: To always express maximum confidence.
options.feedback: Calibration is the accuracy of confidence, not maximal confidence.

options.label: B
options.text: To be right about as often as the confidence you claim, for example correct roughly seventy percent of the time when you say you are seventy percent sure.
options.feedback: Calibration aligns stated confidence with actual hit rate.

options.label: C
options.text: To avoid stating any confidence level.
options.feedback: Calibration requires stating and tracking confidence.

options.label: D
options.text: To match the AI's confidence exactly.
options.feedback: Calibration is about your own accuracy, not copying the model.

prompt: When an AI produces a confident forecast, how do the structured-reasoning frameworks help a team respond?

options.label: A
options.text: They replace the forecast with the team's gut feeling.
options.feedback: The frameworks discipline the forecast rather than replace it with intuition.

options.label: B
options.text: They qualify the forecast by surfacing failure modes, checking it against base rates, and scoring the inputs, producing a recommendation with a band and conditions.
options.feedback: The frameworks turn a raw number into a defensible, qualified recommendation.

options.label: C
options.text: They accept the forecast as long as it is presented confidently.
options.feedback: Confidence is not a reason to accept a forecast unqualified.

options.label: D
options.text: They delay any decision indefinitely.
options.feedback: The aim is a qualified decision, not paralysis.

prompt: What is the Draft-and-Verify workflow?

options.label: A
options.text: Letting the AI both draft and approve the final work.
options.feedback: Verification is the human's job, not the AI's.

options.label: B
options.text: Using AI to produce a fast first draft, which the human then checks and corrects before it is used.
options.feedback: The human verifies and owns the output the AI drafted.

options.label: C
options.text: Writing everything manually and asking AI only for spelling.
options.feedback: That understates the drafting role of AI in this workflow.

options.label: D
options.text: Drafting once and never reviewing.
options.feedback: Verification is the defining second step.

prompt: What is the goal of the Adversarial Review workflow?

options.label: A
options.text: To have the AI praise the strengths of your plan.
options.feedback: Adversarial Review seeks challenges, not praise.

options.label: B
options.text: To have the AI generate the strongest counter-arguments and challenges to your position so you can address them.
options.feedback: It pressure-tests your thinking by surfacing the best objections.

options.label: C
options.text: To speed up drafting.
options.feedback: That is closer to Draft-and-Verify.

options.label: D
options.text: To remove the need for human judgment.
options.feedback: It informs judgment rather than removing it.

prompt: How does the Premortem-with-AI workflow use the model?

options.label: A
options.text: As the source of the final decision.
options.feedback: The AI generates failure modes; the human decides.

options.label: B
options.text: As a rapid generator of plausible failure modes, which the team then triages.
options.feedback: The AI expands the set of failure modes quickly for human triage.

options.label: C
options.text: As a way to avoid running a premortem at all.
options.feedback: It is a way to run one faster, not to skip it.

options.label: D
options.text: As the tool that writes the final memo.
options.feedback: That belongs to a later step, not the premortem.

prompt: A Decision Audit separates decision quality from outcome quality. Why does this distinction matter?

options.label: A
options.text: Because a good outcome always proves the decision was sound.
options.feedback: A good outcome can follow a weak decision through luck.

options.label: B
options.text: Because outcomes are influenced by luck, so a sound decision can have a bad result and a weak decision a good one.
options.feedback: Judging the reasoning, not just the result, is how you learn correctly.

options.label: C
options.text: Because only outcomes can be measured.
options.feedback: The reasoning available at the time can and should be reviewed.

options.label: D
options.text: Because decision quality and outcome quality are the same thing.
options.feedback: The whole point is that they are distinct.

prompt: The Prompt Risk Review screens a prompt against five questions. What is its decision rule?

options.label: A
options.text: Send any prompt as long as it is short.
options.feedback: Length is not part of the rule.

options.label: B
options.text: If the content is confidential, personal, bias-prone, or customer-facing or regulated, and the tool's handling of the input is uncertain, do not send the prompt as drafted.
options.feedback: A yes on a risk question plus uncertainty about the tool stops the send.

options.label: C
options.text: Only legal teams may ever use AI prompts.
options.feedback: The review is a quick daily habit for everyone, not a legal gate.

options.label: D
options.text: Always send the prompt and review it afterward.
options.feedback: The review happens before sending, not after.

prompt: What principle did the Moffatt v. Air Canada decision establish for organizations?

options.label: A
options.text: Chatbots are separate legal entities, so companies are not responsible for them.
options.feedback: The tribunal rejected this argument.

options.label: B
options.text: A company is responsible for what its customer-facing AI tells customers.
options.feedback: The ruling held the company accountable for its chatbot's statements.

options.label: C
options.text: Customers must independently verify everything a chatbot says.
options.feedback: The duty of accuracy rested with the company.

options.label: D
options.text: Statements made by AI are never binding.
options.feedback: The company was held to the commitment its chatbot made.

prompt: Why does a decision journal record reasoning before the outcome is known?

options.label: A
options.text: Because predictions are more accurate when written down.
options.feedback: The purpose is honest capture, not better prediction.

options.label: B
options.text: Because once the result is known, hindsight rewrites our memory of the reasoning, so capturing it early preserves an honest record for calibration.
options.feedback: The journal locks in the reasoning before hindsight distorts it.

options.label: C
options.text: Because it is required before any decision is approved.
options.feedback: It is a learning tool, not an approval gate.

options.label: D
options.text: Because it eliminates the need to track outcomes.
options.feedback: Outcomes are compared against the entry later.

prompt: A defensible decision memo documents the basis of a decision, not just the conclusion. Which elements does it capture?

options.label: A
options.text: Only the final recommendation.
options.feedback: The conclusion alone is not defensible.

options.label: B
options.text: The question, the AI inputs and how they were verified, the evidence, the reasoning, the recommendation, and the residual risks.
options.feedback: These six elements show how the decision was reached.

options.label: C
options.text: The names of everyone who attended the meeting.
options.feedback: Attendance is not the memo's content.

options.label: D
options.text: A list of the tools the team owns.
options.feedback: Tool inventory is not part of the memo.

prompt: At a high level, in what order does the capstone apply the course toolkit?

options.label: A
options.text: Write the memo, then verify inputs, then reason.
options.feedback: Verification and reasoning precede the memo.

options.label: B
options.text: Verify the inputs, then apply reasoning discipline, then construct the defensible memo.
options.feedback: The capstone moves from verification to reasoning to the memo.

options.label: C
options.text: Reason first, then skip verification.
options.feedback: Verification is the first stage, not skipped.

options.label: D
options.text: Construct the memo and skip the premortem.
options.feedback: Reasoning discipline, including the premortem, comes before the memo.

prompt: The thirty-day practice plan introduces one habit per week. What is the intended sequence?

options.label: A
options.text: Decision audit, then journaling, then prompt screening, then verification.
options.feedback: That reverses the build order.

options.label: B
options.text: Input verification, then prompt screening, then decision documentation, then a decision audit.
options.feedback: The plan stages verification, screening, journaling, then a single audit.

options.label: C
options.text: All four habits at once in the first week.
options.feedback: The plan deliberately staggers one habit per week.

options.label: D
options.text: Prompt screening first, then verification, then auditing, then journaling.
options.feedback: Input verification comes first.
